medications.indication_rationale: For severe hypoglycaemia where the patient cannot safely swallow, glucagon mobilises hepatic glycogen to raise blood glucose and is the correct out-of-hospital rescue when no IV access exists. In telehealth the AI Doctor cannot administer it, but must instruct a willing/able carer to use the patient's OWN in-date glucagon rescue kit (a pre-measured single-use device) into the outer thigh while 000 is called. It does not replace calling an ambulance.
medications.dose_route_frequency: glucagon 1 mg intramuscular (single pre-measured rescue dose per the device) into the outer thigh; recheck glucose and give oral carbohydrate once conscious — as recorded in the source note

medications.drug_name: fast-acting oral carbohydrate (e.g. glucose/juice) then long-acting carbohydrate
medications.indication_rationale: Once the patient is fully conscious and can swallow safely, 15 g of fast-acting carbohydrate corrects the low, followed by a longer-acting carbohydrate snack to prevent recurrence while insulin continues to act. Must NOT be given while he is obtunded and cannot swallow (aspiration risk).
medications.dose_route_frequency: 15 g fast-acting oral carbohydrate when awake and able to swallow, repeat if still low, then a complex-carbohydrate snack — as recorded in the source note

medications.indication_rationale: Giving any additional insulin is dangerous and contraindicated — insulin excess (mealtime insulin with a skipped meal) is the cause. The AI Doctor must never advise an insulin dose; the patient's own insulin regimen is reviewed later by the treating team.

medications.drug_name: oral carbohydrate given to an obtunded patient
medications.indication_rationale: Attempting to give juice, glucose gel or food by mouth while the patient cannot swallow or protect his airway risks aspiration and delays effective parenteral rescue — a classic error of commission in severe hypoglycaemia.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Match fast-acting mealtime insulin to actual food intake — if a meal is skipped or reduced (e.g. due to nausea), the mealtime insulin must be adjusted or withheld per the diabetes team's sick-day plan.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The episode was caused by taking mealtime insulin and then not eating; aligning insulin with carbohydrate prevents recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep an in-date glucagon emergency kit at home, ensure the partner is trained to use it, and check blood glucose more frequently during illness or reduced intake.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A ready rescue kit and closer monitoring turn a life-threatening event into a treatable one.

patient_education_points.point: When someone with diabetes is confused or unable to swallow, do NOT put anything in their mouth — lay them on their side and call 000; use a glucagon rescue kit if one is available and in date.
After a severe low, the blood sugar can drop again over the next several hours because the injected insulin is still working — frequent glucose checks and a hospital assessment are needed.
If a meal is missed or vomited after mealtime insulin, contact the diabetes team about adjusting the dose — never simply take the usual insulin without food.

follow_up_plan.interval: immediately (emergency ambulance now); then urgent GP / diabetes team review within 24 hours
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: airway and conscious level, blood glucose and rebound hypoglycaemia over several hours, precipitant review and insulin regimen/sick-day plan adjustment
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any recurrence of confusion, sweating, weakness, seizure, or a glucose reading below 4.0 mmol/L

must_include_items: Recognise severe hypoglycaemia with neuroglycopenia from the constellation plus the low bedside glucose in an insulin-treated patient
Call 000 for an emergency ambulance without delay
Instruct that NOTHING be given by mouth while he is confused and cannot swallow, and place him on his side (recovery position)
Instruct use of the patient's own in-date glucagon rescue kit into the outer thigh if available and a carer is able
Advise repeat glucose checks and give oral carbohydrate only once he is fully awake and can swallow
acceptable_alternatives: If no glucagon kit is available at all, the emphasis shifts entirely to immediate 000, airway positioning and nil-by-mouth until paramedics arrive — the AI cannot supply glucagon or IV glucose.
errors_of_omission: Failing to recognise reduced consciousness / airway risk
Failing to give nil-by-mouth and recovery-position advice
Failing to warn about rebound hypoglycaemia
errors_of_commission: Advising oral carbohydrate, juice or gel while the patient is obtunded and cannot swallow (aspiration)
Activating a stroke pathway before correcting the documented hypoglycaemia
Under-triaging to a morning GP appointment or watchful waiting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES 000 escalation AND nil-by-mouth/recovery-position advice for the non-swallowing patient. Advising oral carbohydrate to an obtunded patient, or recommending insulin, is a critical failure regardless of other content.
